stumpers.question: A professor writes the integers 1, 2, 3, …, 25 on a blackboard. Students take turns: each erases two numbers a and b, then writes |a − b| in their place. After 24 such operations only one number remains. What are ALL possible values of the final remaining number?
stumpers.failure_analysis: The agent correctly identified the parity invariant (sum = 325, each operation preserves parity, so final value must be odd) and the signed-sum framework (final value = |±1 ± 2 ± ... ± 25|). However, it MISSED the critical maximum-value constraint: since |a-b| ≤ max(a,b) for all non-negative integers, the maximum value on the board can never increase beyond the initial maximum of 25. The agent treated it as a pure signed-sum problem and concluded values up to 323 are achievable, when in fact no value > 25 can ever appear on the board.
stumpers.weakness_exploited: The model applies correct mathematical frameworks (signed sums, parity invariants) but fails to check structural/physical constraints imposed by the operation itself (|a-b| ≤ max(a,b) means the max is monotonically non-increasing)

learnings: Claude Opus 4.6 recognizes ALL classic puzzles, riddles, and trick questions from training data
Step-by-step reasoning is extremely reliable — the model rarely makes errors even in long chains (30+ steps)
Documented LLM failure modes (decimal comparison, 'strawberry' counting, base rate neglect) are all patched
The model correctly distinguishes subtle problem variants (e.g., 'meet a specific child' vs 'at least one boy')
Multi-level theory of mind works: the model reasons about what others would know in hypothetical scenarios
Character-level string operations are accurate even for 12+ character words and full-sentence ciphers
Complex arithmetic (4-digit × 4-digit) is handled correctly via algebraic decomposition
Group theory / Burnside's lemma computed correctly with multiple symmetry groups (C₄, D₆, D₈)
Parity invariant arguments are discovered and applied without prompting
20-step Collatz and 30-step tribonacci computations completed without a single arithmetic error
The model knows advanced results (100 prisoners ≈ 31%, ants-on-stick equivalence, n² random walk formula, Penney's game)
Precise counting over 28 items completed without error
Novel game theory problems (non-standard Nim with no-repeat constraint) solved by building complete game trees
Nested modular exponentiation (7^7^7^7 mod 1000) handled with multi-level Euler's theorem application
Matrix permanent (24-term enumeration with multiplication and summation) computed correctly
10-character state tracking through 7 complex operations (insert, remove, reverse, swap) — zero errors
Counterintuitive probability problems (Russian Roulette adjacent bullets, Bertrand's box, Tuesday Boy 13/27) all solved
CUSTOM NOVEL RECURRENCES never seen in training data are computed correctly through 20 steps
The model detects inconsistent premises (impossible triangle) but may still provide a numerical answer due to helpfulness bias
Hook length formula, Legendre's formula, inclusion-exclusion, CRT — all applied correctly without prompting
This model is GENUINELY UNSTUMPABLE on single-turn, well-defined reasoning problems across 90 questions and 18 rounds

FIRST STUMPER FOUND (Q91): Blackboard |a-b| game exploits a constraint-checking blind spot — the model applies the correct mathematical framework but misses that |a-b| ≤ max(a,b) means the max on the board never increases

The exploitable weakness pattern: problems where a mathematical framework (signed sums, invariants) gives a NECESSARY condition, but a STRUCTURAL constraint of the operation imposes a tighter bound that the model overlooks
The model excels at abstract algebra (parity, signed sums, group theory) but can miss concrete operational constraints (monotonicity of max under |a-b|)
